settings.title: Two-story rear addition
settings.summary: Six hundred square feet added to a foursquare — a family room below, a primary suite above, and a roofline that looks like it was always there.
settings.description:
The family loved their street and their neighbors but had outgrown the house. Moving meant leaving both; adding meant getting the details right so the addition didn't announce itself.

Matching a century-old house

We matched the original siding profile, sourced windows with the same sightlines as the 1920s originals, and carried the roof pitch through so the rear elevation reads as one building.

The addition sits on a foundation stepped to match the original, and the water table and corner boards carry through at the same heights. From the street, the only tell is that the paint is newer.

New rooms, same house

Inside, a new family room opens to the garden, and the primary suite upstairs finally gave the parents a door that closes. The suite takes the whole upper floor of the addition: bedroom, a bathroom with a walk-in shower, and a closet that ended a decade of negotiation over one small dresser.

The old back bedroom became a proper laundry and mudroom, which is the change the family says they would rank first, ahead of all six hundred new square feet.

Weathertight before the rains

Framed, roofed, and weathertight before the fall rains; finished in fourteen weeks total. We sequenced the exterior shell first so the family could live normally downstairs through the interior finish work.

The neighbors' verdict — that they couldn't tell where the old house ends — is the one we build for.
